title: Phaca parishii
intro: Phaca parishii, also known as Parish's lupine or lupin, is a perennial legume native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Phaca parishii include alkaloids, such as cytisine and lupanine, as well as flavonoids and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Phaca parishii is commonly used in herbal preparations, such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, to treat fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, as well as to support cardiovascular health.
taxonomy: Phaca parishii is a species of flowering plant that belongs to the legume family, Fabaceae. It is a part of the subfamily Faboideae. Within this subfamily, Phaca parishii is classified under the tribe Genisteae. The genus Phaca is a small genus of flowering plants, native to Europe and Western Asia. Phaca parishii is native to the southwestern United States.
